perturbation.original_text: In the ev ent of loss or theft of any documents, items of work in progress, or any work products embodying Material, the Consortium Secretariat must be notified immediately by the Receiving Party, who shall cooperate fully in remedying same.
perturbation.changed_text: In the ev ent of loss or theft of any documents, items of work in progress, or any work products embodying Material, the Consortium Secretariat must be notified within 30 days of discovery by the Receiving Party, who shall cooperate fully in remedying same.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' for reporting loss or theft of confidential material creates an unreasonable delay. Under breach notification laws, such delays could be non-compliant.
perturbation.contradicted_law: GDPR Article 33: Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 33 of the GDPR requires that a personal data breach be notified to the supervisory authority without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. The modified text contradicts this by allowing for notification up to 30 days after discovery, which is well beyond the GDPR's timeframe.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 33 GDPR – Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55 , unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. 2 Where the notification to the supervisory authority is not made within 72 hours, it shall be accompanied by reasons for the delay. The processor shall notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. The notification referred to in paragraph 1 shall at least: describe the nature of the personal data breach including where possible, the categories and approximate number of data subjects concerned and the categories and approximate number of personal data records concerned; communicate the name and contact details of the data protection officer or other contact point where more information can be obtained; describe the likely consequences of the personal data breach; describe the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the controller to address the personal data breach, including, where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects. Where, and in so far as, it is not possible to provide the information at the same time, the information may be provided in phases without undue further delay. 1 The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. 2 That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article. Suitable Recitals ( 85 ) Notification Obligation of Breaches to the Supervisory Authority ( 87 ) Promp
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification timeframe from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days,' which directly contradicts Article 33 of the GDPR. The scraped law snippet states that a personal data breach must be notified to the supervisory authority 'without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it.' Allowing 30 days violates this requirement, making the modified contract non-compliant with GDPR's mandated rapid notification.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as all the material disclosed hereunder becomes public ly known pursuant to Section 15 of the Consortium Agreement and made generally available without vi olation of this Agreement, or (i i) twelve (12) years from date of Registration.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as all the material disclosed hereunder becomes public ly known pursuant to Section 15 of the Consortium Agreement and made generally available without vi olation of this Agreement, or (i i) fifty (50) years from date of Registration.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality term to 50 years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under trade secret laws, especially if the information loses its commercial value much sooner. Many jurisdictions consider excessively long confidentiality periods to be against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't specify a maximum duration for trade secret protection, it implies that protection lasts only as long as the information remains a trade secret and provides a competitive advantage. A 50-year confidentiality term, regardless of the information's actual lifespan as a trade secret, could be challenged as unreasonable and contrary to the principles of trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the confidentiality term from 12 to 50 years. According to the Law Explanation, a 50-year term could be challenged as unreasonable and contrary to trade secret law principles. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating that such a long term may be unenforceable, especially if the information loses commercial value sooner. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction: 'when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.' Although the snippets are missing, the Law and Perturbation explanations provide enough context to determine that the modified text likely creates an unenforceable deadline.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or improper use of the Material, to prevent the material from falling into the public domain, and to protect it from falling into the possession of Third Parties. Such measures include, but shall not be limited to, the highest de gree of care that the Receiving Party utilises to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall take only minimal measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or improper use of the Material. Such measures include, but shall not be limited to, the lowest de gree of care that the Receiving Party utilises to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the required protection to 'minimal measures' and the 'lowest degree of care' contradicts the standard of reasonable measures required to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Trade Secrets Act Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Specifying 'minimal measures' and the 'lowest degree of care' fails to meet the standard of reasonable efforts, thus jeopardizing trade secret protection.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly reduces the protection of trade secrets from 'all reasonable measures' and 'highest degree of care' to 'minimal measures' and the 'lowest degree of care'. This directly contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), implying a legal standard for trade secret protection, which the modified text fails to meet.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Governing Law and disputes This Agreement shall be governed by, and all dis putes arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved in accordance with the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Governing Law and disputes This Agreement shall be governed by, and all dis putes arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved in accordance with the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law as the governing law in a contract involving international commerce and parties from other countries may be deemed unenforceable due to public policy concerns and potential conflicts with international legal standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the law governing their contract, but this choice is not absolute. A court may refuse to apply the chosen law if it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Choosing North Korean law, given its legal system's divergence from international norms, likely violates public policy in many jurisdictions, rendering the choice unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the laws of North Korea govern the agreement, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation clarifies that Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the governing law, but this choice is not absolute. Courts can reject the chosen law if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. The Perturbation Explanation notes that North Korean law may be unenforceable due to public policy concerns and conflicts with international legal standards. Thus, selecting North Korean law likely violates public policy in many jurisdictions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. Choosing North Korean law introduces a significant conflict of laws issue, especially if other jurisdictions have a stronger connection to the contract or parties involved. Given the divergence of North Korean law from international norms, enforcing it could contradict fundamental legal policies, thus creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be executed in a number of c ounterparts, which shall together constitute a single agreement. Each undersigned Receiving Party shall execute two (2) signature pages, retain one for its file and communicate th e other to the Consortium Secretariat.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be executed orally. There will be no written record of signatures or agreement details.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring an agreement regarding confidential information to be executed orally undermines legal enforceability, especially concerning intellectual property and trade secrets, which typically require written documentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds, present in various forms in many jurisdictions, generally requires certain types of contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. While the exact scope varies, agreements that cannot be performed within one year often fall under this requirement. An oral agreement with no written record makes it difficult to prove the terms of the agreement, its duration, and the obligations of each party, especially in intellectual property and trade secret contexts, thus significantly weakening enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Statute of Frauds: Purpose, Contracts It Covers, and Exceptions Table of Contents Expand Table of Contents What Is the Statute of Frauds? History How It Works Requirements Exceptions Examples Explain Like I'm Five FAQs The Bottom Line Investopedia / Dennis Madamba Close Definition The statute of frauds is a legal principle that requires certain contracts to be executed in writing. What Is the Statute of Frauds? The statute of frauds is a legal doctrine requiring that certain types of contracts be in written form. The most common contracts covered by the statute of frauds include the sale of land, agreements involving goods worth $500 or more, and contracts lasting one year or more. The purpose of the statute of frauds is to prevent fraud or other injury. These purposes are often described as being evidentiary and cautionary. The evidentiary function of the statute of frauds is to provide documentation that a legal, binding agreement exists. The cautionary function of the statute of frauds is meant to make each party more intent, serious, and deliberate in their transacting. Statutes of fraud were adopted in the U.S. primarily as a common law concept—that is, as unwritten law. However, many states have formalized the concept by creating statutes. In a breach of contract case where the statute of frauds applies, the defendant may raise it as a defense where the burden of proof is on the plaintiff. The plaintiff must establish that a valid contract was indeed in existence. Key Takeaways The statute of frauds is a common law concept that requires written contracts for certain agreements to be binding. The statute applies to land sales and most purchases of goods of $500 or more. There are significant exceptions, such as oral contracts where work has already started. The statute of frauds varies somewhat between states in the United States. Many aspects of the statute of frauds are included in the Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts. History of the Statute of Frauds The statute of frauds has its roots in the Act for Prevention of Frauds and Perjuryes, which was passed by the English Parliament in 1677. The legislation, which stipulated that a written contract be used for transactions where a large amount of money was at stake, aimed to prevent some of the misunderstandings and fraudulent activity that can occur when relying on oral contracts . Indeed, the English legal system of the time suffered from a lack of written evidence. The courts were clogged with lawsuits, and cases were often settled by using professional witnesses who were paid for their testimony. Perjury and corruption became the norm. As the founders shaped the U.S. government, they drew on the 1677 Act to help shape how business transactions, and disputes over them, should be handled in the new world. Like their 17th-century British forebears, the founders decided that written and signed contracts minimized ambiguity by providing a clear record of the agreement. That reduced the opportunity for later litigation and simplified the settlement of such suits when they occurred. Contracts Covered by the Statute of Frauds As applied in the United States, the statute of frauds generally requires the following types of contracts to be written to be legally binding. Any promises made in connection with marriage, including such gifts as an engagement ring. Contracts that cannot be completed in less than one year. Contracts for the sale of land—leases need not be covered unless they are for a year or more. Promises to pay an estate’s debt from the personal funds of the executor. However, promises to pay such debt from the estate's funds are not subject to the statute of frauds. Contracts for the sale of goods above a specific dollar amount, typically $500. A contract where one person promises to pay the debt of another person. A contract for the sale of personal property not otherwise covered, to the extent of enforcement by way of action or defense beyond $ 5,000 in
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction because it stipulates that the agreement will be executed orally, with no written record. This directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires "certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract" to prevent fraud. Scraped Law Snippet 2 further elaborates that the statute of frauds requires certain types of contracts to be executed in writing, and its evidentiary function is "to provide documentation that a legal, binding agreement exists." The modified text eliminates this written requirement, thereby undermining the enforceability of the agreement and creating a legally vulnerable situation.
